source_title: Exclusive: OpenAI Losses Increased Nearly 8X in 2025, With Spending Hitting $34 Billion
other_side: This is the very leak NLW builds half his episode on — Zitron is the one who obtained and published OpenAI's audited 2025 figures, verified by the Financial Times. He reads them as terminal where NLW reads them as misunderstood. By his account OpenAI took $13.07 billion in revenue against $34 billion in total costs and a $20.92 billion operating loss, with research and development alone at $19.18 billion. He stresses that operating losses are 'mounting year-over-year at a dramatic rate' and says plainly he is 'not sure how this company finds a way toward any kind of sustainability or profitability.' Where NLW lifts out the inference line — $13 billion of revenue on $7.5 billion cost of revenue — as the overlooked good-news story, Zitron treats it as a rounding detail beside a loss from operations that dwarfs every dollar of revenue.
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether OpenAI's headline loss is a fundamentally unprofitable business or accounting noise sitting on top of a viable core. They accept the same audited facts — $13.07bn revenue, $7.5bn cost of revenue, $34bn total costs, a $20.92bn operating loss, and a one-time $41.55bn fair-value charge from the for-profit conversion that inflates the $38.5bn headline. The empirical question that resolves it is whether, once that one-off charge is stripped out, the operating loss narrows as inference gross profit scales faster than R&D and sales grow; the observable is OpenAI's next audited statement or IPO prospectus showing the ex-one-off operating-margin trajectory. The residue is pure worldview: is the gap between cost and value structural, or a phase of investment ahead of a real engine? NLW is vindicated if the inference profit scales and operating losses shrink ex the conversion charge; Zitron if losses keep mounting with no margin path. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW anti-doom and reaching for the bullish read, Zitron bubble-certain and the man who chose to publish the leak — yet both accept the same numbers and even agree the $13bn-on-$7.5bn inference line is real; they differ only on whether it matters. Watch the next audited disclosure and whether the operating loss, stripped of the conversion charge, actually falls.
